variants.h1: Astro Template for Developer Blogs
variants.metaTitle: Astro Developer Blog Template (Free + Pro) | ClaudeStack
variants.metaDescription: Launch your developer blog in minutes with an Astro 6 template that scores 100/100 on Lighthouse. MDX, RSS, dark mode, OG images. Free on GitHub or Pro at $49.
variants.intro: Most developer blog templates ship looking impressive in a screenshot and then break the moment you try to write a fifth post. ClaudeStack was built backwards from that problem. The author wrote thirty thousand words of real content on the template before publishing it, fixing every paper cut along the way: the broken RSS feed, the slow first-paint, the OG image that doesn't render on LinkedIn, the search that only works for the first five articles. What you get is a developer blog that ships clean from the start: MDX with syntax highlighting, automatic Open Graph images, full-text search, and a design that holds up at length. No theme bloat. No framework lock-in beyond Astro itself. No editor that fights your terminal-based writing flow.
useCases: An engineer publishing weekly notes on what they learned shipping production code. Long-form, code-heavy, no fluff. Posts that earn search traffic from real problems rather than viral takes that age badly.
A team blog where each engineer drops in postmortems, design decisions, and tooling tips. Markdown commits feel native to the engineering workflow; no CMS round-trip kills the cadence.

A side project where the blog is half of the marketing. Indie hackers ship a product, write three or four detailed essays about the build, and let those essays do the user acquisition while the founder works on the next feature.
features: MDX content with Shiki syntax highlighting in github-dark theme
Dynamic Open Graph images generated per article at build time (Satori)
Pagefind full-text search — no Algolia subscription required
RSS feed auto-generated from your content collection
100/100 Lighthouse out of the box, before you write a line of CSS

faq.q: Do I need to know Astro to use this?
faq.a: If you can write Markdown and read TypeScript, you can use ClaudeStack on day one. The template ships with a single configuration file (src/config/site.ts) where you set your name, social links, and navigation. Writing posts means dropping .mdx files in src/content/blog/. No Astro-specific knowledge needed for the basics. If you want to customize components, light JSX-style Astro syntax is approachable for anyone who's used React or Vue.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Hugo / Jekyll / Next.js?
faq.a: Yes. The content is plain Markdown / MDX with frontmatter, the same format Hugo and Jekyll use. The migration script in the docs shows how to copy your existing posts in, normalize the frontmatter, and run the first build. Migrations of 50-100 posts typically take an afternoon. Migrating from Next.js MDX is even easier because the file format is identical.

faq.q: What's the difference between the free and Pro version?
faq.a: The free version on GitHub gives you one theme (terminal), basic components, and five demo articles. It's a complete, deployable blog. The Pro version adds three themes with a switcher, premium components (pricing page, comparison tables, code-tabs), full documentation with video walk-throughs, and 30-day email support if you get stuck. Most people who buy Pro do so because the time saved on setup exceeds $49.

faq.q: Will my blog rank on Google?
faq.a: The template gives you every technical SEO box checked: server-rendered HTML, semantic markup, Schema.org Article markup, sitemap, RSS feed, OG images, fast page loads. What it can't give you is content people want to read or backlinks from other sites. Those are still your job. But if you write well, the template won't be what's holding you back from ranking.

variants.ctaCopy: Start with the free version on GitHub. Upgrade to Pro when you want three themes, premium components, and the docs that save you an afternoon of fiddling.

variants.h1: Astro Blog Template for AI Engineers
variants.metaTitle: Astro Blog Template for AI Engineers | ClaudeStack
variants.metaDescription: A dev blog template built for AI engineers: prompt logs, RAG case studies, embedded notebooks, terminal aesthetic. 100/100 Lighthouse. Free on GitHub.
variants.intro: AI engineering writing has a specific shape: code blocks with prompts, model outputs that need to render verbatim, comparison tables for benchmarks, embedded reasoning traces. Most blog templates treat all this as generic Markdown and the result is unreadable. ClaudeStack was built by someone shipping production agentic code, so the choices reflect what that writing actually looks like: monospace by default, JetBrains Mono everywhere, a code block component that handles multi-line prompts without breaking, and prose styling that doesn't fight against tables of model evaluations. If you publish notes on RAG architectures, prompt engineering patterns, or agent orchestration, the template gets out of the way and lets the code-dense writing breathe.
useCases: Publishing prompt patterns that other engineers can copy verbatim. Wide code blocks, no auto-wrap, copy-button on every snippet. Readers paste into their own session and confirm it works.

Documenting a multi-agent system architecture. Diagrams ship as inline SVG, system prompts go in MDX code fences, and the article structure (TOC + headings + related posts) helps readers navigate a long technical breakdown.
Tracking model evaluations across releases. Comparison tables render cleanly, dates are part of the standard meta line, and the RSS feed lets other AI engineers subscribe to your benchmark updates.
features: Code blocks built for long prompts and model outputs — no truncation, no broken wrapping
JetBrains Mono variable font for any text that looks better in mono
MDX so you can drop custom React-style components (charts, prompt cards) into prose
Reading time + word count meta on every article — relevant for technical content
Schema.org Article + FAQ markup — what AI search engines parse to surface your content

faq.q: Can I embed Jupyter notebook output?
faq.a: The template doesn't ship a Jupyter renderer because that adds dependencies most readers don't need. But MDX lets you paste notebook output as code blocks (the export-to-markdown option in Jupyter), and rich plots can be exported as PNG and dropped in with Astro's image optimization. For full interactive notebooks, embed a JupyterLite iframe — the template's responsive grid handles it cleanly.

faq.q: Does it support LaTeX / math notation?
faq.a: Out of the box, no. KaTeX or MathJax can be added with a single Astro integration (`astro add remark-math rehype-katex`). The template's CSS reset doesn't fight math rendering — equations display correctly once the integration is enabled. The setup adds about 80KB of CSS to pages that use math; the docs explain how to scope it to only those pages.

faq.q: Will it handle hundreds of articles with thousands of code blocks?
faq.a: Yes. Static generation means the build cost grows linearly with article count and code-block count, but runtime cost stays flat — every page is pre-rendered HTML. A test with 500 articles, each averaging 1500 words and 6 code blocks, built in under 45 seconds and served at the same speed as a 5-article site. The bottleneck is your build server, not the architecture.

faq.q: Is there a comments system for technical discussion?
faq.a: The template ships with a placeholder. Plug in Giscus (GitHub Discussions-based) or Disqus by editing one component — both work without changing the template's core. Giscus is the more popular choice for technical blogs because discussions live alongside your code in GitHub and require less moderation than a generic comment system.

variants.ctaCopy: Clone the free version and write your first prompt-engineering essay this weekend. Upgrade to Pro when you want themes, premium components, and direct support.

variants.h1: Astro Blog Template for ML Engineers
variants.metaTitle: Astro Blog Template for Machine Learning Engineers | ClaudeStack
variants.metaDescription: Publish ML experiments, paper notes, and training logs on an Astro template that handles long-form technical content. MDX, code-heavy, 100/100 Lighthouse.
variants.intro: ML engineering writing is half ablation tables and half walls of code. Most blog templates were built for lifestyle bloggers, and it shows the moment you try to publish your first experiment log: tables wrap badly, code blocks have line numbers that don't copy, and the typography fights you on dense technical sections. ClaudeStack starts from the opposite premise. The default theme is terminal-flavored mono, tables render at full code-block width without breaking, and the article layout assumes you have something long and structured to say. Whether you're posting paper notes, training run summaries, or open-source model releases, the template hands you readable prose without you having to overhaul the CSS first.
useCases: Paper-of-the-week notes. Each entry has a structured summary section, three key takeaways, and a code snippet that reimplements the core idea. The template's article layout gives you the structure without you having to design it yourself.

Training run logs published as you go. Date-stamped posts, tagged by model and dataset, RSS so colleagues subscribe to your experimental cadence. The tag pages auto-generate by model name and let readers filter to what they care about.

Open-source model release announcements. Long-form, code-heavy, with benchmark tables, training cost numbers, and links to the Hugging Face repo. The Schema.org Article markup helps these rank when people search for your model name.
features: Markdown tables render at full code-block width — ablation results look readable
Configurable code-block themes via Shiki (github-dark default; switch in two lines)
Per-article custom OG images with the model/experiment name auto-baked in
Tag and category pages auto-generated; filter by `paper-notes`, `experiment`, `tooling`
Built-in support for long reading-time articles — the TOC and related-articles surface help

faq.q: Can I display interactive Plotly / matplotlib charts?
faq.a: Plotly charts work via embedded iframes or via the official Plotly JS bundle, dropped into an MDX file. The template's content grid is generous enough that Plotly's default sizing renders without overflow. Matplotlib output is best exported as a static PNG/SVG; Astro's image optimization keeps the page weight under control. For very heavy interactive dashboards, consider linking out to a hosted Streamlit/Gradio instance rather than embedding.

faq.q: How do I version posts when an experiment gets re-run with new data?
faq.a: The frontmatter supports both `pubDate` and `updatedDate`. Update the `updatedDate` and the article footer reflects the change. Schema.org `dateModified` is automatically updated so Google sees the freshness signal. For major revisions where the conclusion changes, the convention is to publish a follow-up post and cross-link rather than silently rewriting the original — both for honesty and for SEO continuity.

faq.q: Does the template handle very long articles well (5000+ words)?
faq.a: Yes. The article layout is designed for long-form: 720px max width for readable line length, sticky-optional TOC, prose styles tuned for 14-16px body text. Tested with 6000-word articles containing 20+ code blocks and a dozen tables. The Pagefind search indexes the full content of each long article so deep references stay discoverable.

faq.q: Can I publish drafts that only I can see?
faq.a: Yes. Set `draft: true` in the frontmatter and the article is excluded from production builds, the RSS feed, the sitemap, and all list pages. In dev mode (`npm run dev`), drafts render so you can preview. The standard workflow is: write in a draft state, review, flip to `draft: false`, commit, push.

variants.ctaCopy: Get the free version, publish your first paper note this week, and let the template do the typography while you focus on the math.

variants.h1: Astro Blog Template for DevOps & SRE Engineers
variants.metaTitle: Astro Blog Template for DevOps Engineers | ClaudeStack
variants.metaDescription: An Astro template tuned for incident postmortems, runbooks, and infrastructure essays. Long-form, code-heavy, RSS-first, 100/100 Lighthouse. Free on GitHub.
variants.intro: DevOps writing is documentation that earns its keep when something is on fire at 3am. The shape: configuration snippets, terminal sessions verbatim, deployment timelines, postmortem timelines, runbook checklists. ClaudeStack was built by an engineer who spends a lot of time staring at the same kinds of artifacts and wanted a blog that didn't fight them. The terminal-default theme means YAML and HCL render in colors that match your actual terminal. Code blocks preserve indentation, support multi-line shell history, and don't auto-format your `kubectl` output into something prettier-but-wrong. If you write incident retrospectives, infra-as-code essays, or how-I-actually-deploy guides, this is a template that respects the work.
useCases: Internal-team postmortem blog. Each incident gets a structured writeup: timeline, root cause, action items, status update on each action item. The template's article structure carries the load; you focus on the content, not the layout.

Public runbook collection. A post per common operation: rotating a secret, scaling a database, deploying a hotfix. Tagged by service, searchable via Pagefind, with the RSS feed for engineers who want to know when new runbooks land.

Infrastructure-as-code essays. Long-form writeups on why your Terraform looks the way it does, what failed about the previous design, what you'd do differently. The kind of writing that gets a paragraph quoted in a Hacker News thread and brings in 50 senior engineers as readers.
features: Terminal-default theme — YAML, HCL, Bash, Dockerfile all render in familiar colors
Built-in tag pages — auto-organize by service, by tool, by incident severity
RSS feed your on-call team can plug into Slack or PagerDuty for runbook updates
Article schema with `dateModified` so updated runbooks show as fresh in search
Per-article OG images — incidents linked in Slack get a clear visual context card

faq.q: Can I gate certain runbooks behind authentication?
faq.a: The template ships as a fully public static site by design. Gating individual pages requires either a server-side layer (a Cloudflare Worker checking JWT, for example) or a separate internal-only deployment. Most teams use the public template for shareable runbooks and writeups, then keep truly sensitive ops docs in their internal wiki. The bookkeeping cost of mixing public and private content in one repo usually outweighs the convenience.

faq.q: Does it integrate with Mermaid diagrams for architecture writeups?
faq.a: Yes. Add the `astro-mermaid` integration and you can embed Mermaid blocks in MDX. The diagrams render server-side at build time so visitors get static SVG without a JS payload. For very complex diagrams, exporting from Excalidraw or draw.io to SVG and dropping in `` keeps the build fast and avoids a Mermaid dependency entirely.

faq.q: Can the same content power both my blog and my internal docs?
faq.a: Two ways. Either run two builds from the same content collection with different configs (the `draft` flag controls public visibility), or use Astro's `getCollection()` filters to gate posts by a `internal: true` flag and deploy two domains from the same repo. The architecture is flexible enough that you don't have to maintain two parallel content trees.

faq.q: How do I handle code blocks that need to update when infrastructure changes?
faq.a: MDX supports importing files. For Terraform snippets or config samples that should always match the deployed reality, import them from your actual infra repo via a git submodule or a build-time fetch. The template's code-block component renders imported content the same way as inline blocks, with syntax highlighting and copy-button intact.

variants.ctaCopy: Clone the free version, publish your first postmortem this week, and stop apologizing for how your internal wiki looks.

ClaudeStack removes the setup tasks. You install once, ship once, then writing a new post is opening a .mdx file in your editor. No CMS, no plugins, no dashboard. The template is opinionated about being out of the way, so the only thing standing between you and post six is opening your terminal. Built-in support for Buttondown means you can capture emails from your readers from day one, which is the only growth lever indie products consistently care about.
useCases: Monthly MRR updates with the actual chart embedded as an SVG. Readers subscribe to the RSS for the updates; the OG image generator gives each post a clear visual when shared on X.

Launch announcements with feature walkthroughs. Code samples render cleanly for the dev audience; screenshots embed at full container width. The product card on the homepage drives readers to your Gumroad / Lemon Squeezy listing.

features: Buttondown newsletter form pre-wired — set your username in config, done
Product card component for the homepage — shows your headline product with pricing
OG images that look professional in X / LinkedIn / Reddit shares
Comments-ready (Giscus / Disqus drop-in) for build-in-public engagement
Zero ongoing cost on Cloudflare — your blog never costs more than its domain

faq.q: How fast can I actually launch?
faq.a: From `git clone` to first deploy on Cloudflare Pages: about 20 minutes if you've used Astro before, an hour if you haven't. The longest step is filling in the site config (name, tagline, social links) thoughtfully — the rest is `npm install`, `npm run dev`, write one post, push. The template makes no assumptions that slow you down; there's no database, no auth, no CMS to configure.

faq.q: Can I display my product's MRR / users / stats live?
faq.a: Two patterns. For relatively static numbers (updated weekly), edit them in the site config and rebuild — Cloudflare's free tier handles 500 builds/month, which is more than enough. For truly live numbers, embed a small JavaScript component that fetches from your own API. The template's `<StatsGrid />` component can take static or dynamic data and the design works for both.

faq.q: Will this work alongside my main product site?
faq.a: Subdomain (blog.yourproduct.com) is the most common setup — keep your product on its main domain, host the blog separately. Both deploy independently. The template's canonical-URL handling and Schema.org markup are correctly configured for subdomain use. If you'd rather have /blog as a path on your main domain, that's a Cloudflare routing config away.

faq.q: Is there a way to A/B test landing copy on the homepage?
faq.a: Not natively — the template is static. For A/B testing, the standard pattern is to use Cloudflare Workers to serve different variants at the edge, or use a tool like PostHog Feature Flags. The template's homepage components accept props, so once you have a flag, swapping copy is one prop change. Most indie hackers find this overkill for their stage; ship one good variant first.

variants.ctaCopy: Ship the free version with your next launch. Upgrade to Pro when you want the three-theme switcher and the premium pricing-page component.

features: Founder-friendly: single config file, no CMS, no contractors needed to publish
Looks like an engineering company built it — credibility signal to dev hires
Multi-author support via the `author` frontmatter field — for guest posts from your team
Mailing list integration ready (Buttondown / ConvertKit / EmailOctopus) — capture investor and customer interest

faq.q: Can multiple team members contribute?
faq.a: Yes — anyone with write access to the GitHub repo can publish. The standard workflow is: write a post in a feature branch, open a PR, get a review (typo check, fact check), merge to main. Cloudflare auto-deploys on merge. For non-technical contributors, GitHub's web editor handles MDX files fine, or you can use Decap CMS as a thin authoring layer over the same content.

faq.q: How does this handle our brand identity?
faq.a: All colors, fonts, and copy live in two files: `src/config/site.ts` (text content) and `src/styles/themes.css` (colors). Both are documented inline. Changing the primary color across the entire site is a one-line edit. If you have a brand guide, the template's tokens can be remapped to your specific palette in about ten minutes. Custom fonts via @fontsource are equally straightforward.

faq.q: Is it SOC 2 / GDPR friendly?
faq.a: The template ships with no analytics, no third-party scripts, no cookies. That makes the privacy story very simple: there's nothing to disclose because nothing is collected. When you add analytics (Cloudflare Web Analytics is the privacy-friendly default the template integrates with), you add one cookie-less script tag. For GDPR, the simpler the stack, the simpler the compliance — and this template starts simpler than any CMS-based alternative.

faq.q: Can we use this for investor updates that should not be public?
faq.a: The template is built for public content. For private investor updates, the right tool is a separate private blog (run the same template on a different Cloudflare project, password-protected via Access). Mixing public and private content in one site usually causes operational mistakes; the cost of running two instances is near zero and the separation is worth it.

variants.ctaCopy: Free version on GitHub is enough to launch your founder blog this week. Pro adds the themes and components you'll want when the readership grows.

Long-form deep-dives into specific tools or concepts. The article layout assumes long-form: TOC, reading time, related articles, FAQ accordion. Designed for content that earns its keep over years, not days.

features: Article layout designed for long-form: TOC, reading time, related posts, FAQ
Prose styles tuned for technical writing — generous line-height, readable code blocks
MDX so you can drop in custom callouts, diagrams, or interactive components
Tag and category pages auto-generated — organize a large archive without manual lift

faq.q: How does this compare to GitBook or Mintlify for documentation?
faq.a: GitBook and Mintlify are stronger if you're publishing API reference documentation with sidebar navigation, multi-version support, and developer-portal features. ClaudeStack is stronger for long-form editorial: tutorials, essays, deep-dives, opinion pieces. The right choice depends on whether your content is reference (use GitBook) or narrative (use this). Some teams run both — reference docs in GitBook, editorial content on a ClaudeStack blog, linked together.

faq.q: Can I have multiple book-length series of articles?
faq.a: Yes. The content collection supports tags and categories, so a 12-part series can be tagged `series:react-internals` and you can build a series-index page that lists them in order. The template doesn't ship a `<Series />` component out of the box, but the documentation includes a 60-line example you can drop in. Once added, every article in the series shows previous/next navigation.

faq.q: Does it support multiple authors with bio pages?
faq.a: The `author` frontmatter field is supported per article. For author bio pages, the template includes an `/about` page tied to `siteConfig.author`. For multi-author setups, the convention is to add an `authors/` folder with a `.md` file per author, then build a dynamic `/authors/[slug]` route that lists their articles. The pattern is documented and takes about 30 minutes to set up.

faq.q: Can my client / publisher review drafts without a developer?
faq.a: Set `draft: true` on the article and it stays hidden from production. For reviewers without GitHub access, deploy a separate preview environment on Cloudflare Pages (a second project pointing at a `preview` branch). Reviewers see your drafts at preview.yoursite.com. Once approved, merge to main and the article goes live. This is the standard editorial workflow used by teams shipping with Astro.

variants.ctaCopy: Start free with the template, write your first long-form piece, and ship it on a domain that doesn't make you embarrassed.

Comparison-heavy reviews (tools, frameworks, services). MDX lets you import a `<ComparisonTable />` component once and reuse it across many articles with varied data.
Long-form essays with callouts, asides, footnotes. Components like `<Callout type='warning'>` let you visually break up prose without inline HTML hell.
features: MDX as the default content format (configured in src/content.config.ts)
Shiki syntax highlighting inside MDX code fences (200+ languages supported)
Import components directly into MDX files — no special setup per post

faq.q: Should I use MDX or stay with plain Markdown?
faq.a: MDX is a superset of Markdown — everything that works in plain Markdown works in MDX. There's no downside to using MDX for files that don't currently need components; you can sprinkle them in later. Most teams start with plain Markdown-flavored MDX and graduate to embedded components when they have a clear use case. The template handles both indistinguishably.

faq.q: Will MDX slow down my build?
faq.a: MDX adds a small parsing cost per file (typically 5-20ms per article). For blogs with hundreds of posts, you'll notice a few seconds added to the build. For typical sizes (under 50 posts), the cost is invisible. The runtime cost is zero — MDX is compiled to plain HTML at build time and served as static files.

faq.q: Can I use React components in MDX?
faq.a: Yes, via Astro's React integration (`npx astro add react`). The template doesn't ship with React enabled (to keep the dependency tree light), but enabling it is one command. Once enabled, you can import React components and use them inside MDX files. They'll be server-rendered or hydrated based on the `client:` directive you use.

faq.q: How does this compare to MDX in Next.js?
faq.a: Functionally similar — same MDX syntax, same component import patterns. Astro's MDX is typically faster at build time because Astro defaults to zero JavaScript on the client. Next.js MDX ships React runtime to the client even for static content; Astro doesn't unless you opt in. For content-heavy sites, Astro's MDX yields lighter pages by default.

variants.ctaCopy: MDX is on by default in the free template. Clone, write your first MDX-enhanced post, and ship before the weekend ends.

variants.intro: Sitemap.xml is the file Google reads to know which of your pages exist. Without one, Google crawls what it discovers via internal links; with one, you tell it explicitly: here are all my pages, including the ones you might not find on your own. ClaudeStack ships sitemap generation via @astrojs/sitemap, configured correctly out of the box. Every blog post, every category page, every tag page, every static route appears in the sitemap automatically. You don't think about it; you just keep writing. When you submit the sitemap URL to Google Search Console, indexing kicks in faster and more reliably than relying on Google's organic crawl alone.
useCases: Any blog targeting Google traffic — sitemap submission is the standard first SEO step after launch. Without it, indexing is slower and incomplete.
Sites with many tag or category pages that won't be linked from the homepage. The sitemap ensures Google finds these long-tail pages even when they're not in the navigation.
Multi-author or multi-section sites where the navigation can't show every page. Sitemap = comprehensive map; navigation = curated highlights. Both matter.
features: @astrojs/sitemap auto-generated on every build — no manual maintenance
Includes all dynamic routes (blog posts, category pages, tag pages)
Excludes draft content automatically (respects the `draft: true` flag)
Sitemap index file links to individual sitemaps for large sites (10,000+ URLs)
Last-modified dates included — helps Google prioritize freshly-updated content

faq.q: How often does Google re-crawl after submitting the sitemap?
faq.a: Typically 3-14 days for the first full crawl. After that, Google re-checks the sitemap roughly weekly and crawls updated URLs based on freshness signals (the `<lastmod>` field). You can manually request re-indexing of specific URLs via Search Console for important updates. The template's sitemap auto-updates `<lastmod>` based on each article's `updatedDate`.

faq.q: Do I need to ping Google when I publish?
faq.a: Not in 2026. Google deprecated the explicit sitemap ping endpoint. Now Google re-checks your sitemap on its own schedule. For urgent indexing of important new posts, use the URL Inspection tool in Search Console to request indexing manually — it's the only ping mechanism that still exists.

faq.q: Will my robots.txt block the sitemap?
faq.a: Only if you explicitly disallow `/sitemap-index.xml` or the URLs it lists, which would be unusual. The template's default robots.txt allows everything and explicitly points to the sitemap location. As long as you don't customize robots.txt to block crawling, the sitemap works as intended.

faq.q: Can I exclude certain pages from the sitemap?
faq.a: Yes. The @astrojs/sitemap config accepts a `filter` function that lets you exclude routes by URL pattern. The template uses this to exclude `/demo/*` paths from the sitemap (since demo content is `noindex` anyway). For your own exclusions, edit `astro.config.mjs` and add the routes you want hidden from search engines.

variants.ctaCopy: Free version generates a sitemap automatically. Submit it to Google Search Console after your first deploy.

variants.h1: Astro Blog Template with Full-Text Search

variants.intro: Search on a static site used to mean either no search at all or an Algolia subscription. ClaudeStack uses Pagefind — a fully static search engine that builds its index from your generated HTML and runs entirely in the visitor's browser. No backend, no API key, no monthly subscription, no rate limits. The index gets generated as part of your build step; visitors download a small chunk on demand when they start typing. Search is instant and accurate, with results that match technical terms (camelCase, snake_case, dot.notation) the way developers expect. For most blogs, this is the right answer for the next decade.
useCases: Blogs with 30+ articles where readers want to find specific topics fast. Pagefind handles this scale effortlessly with no infrastructure cost.
Reference-style content (tutorials, runbooks, deep-dives) where readers often search instead of browse. The search UI is small and unobtrusive — sits on the blog index page, ready when needed.
Multi-language sites (Pagefind supports per-language indexes). Each language gets its own index, with proper stopword handling and stemming.
features: Pagefind static search — runs entirely client-side, no backend required
Index built automatically as part of `npm run build` (chained in package.json)
Supports highlighted excerpts in results — readers see the matching context
~50KB total index size for a 30-post blog — minimal payload
Respects content collection boundaries — searches blog only, not demo articles

faq.q: How does Pagefind compare to Algolia?
faq.a: Algolia is stronger for very large sites (10,000+ pages), faceted search, and complex ranking customization. Pagefind is stronger for blogs and documentation sites under 5,000 pages — same result quality, zero backend cost, no monthly subscription. For most personal and small-team blogs, Pagefind is the right answer; you'll never grow into a need for Algolia.

faq.q: Does search work in dev mode?
faq.a: Pagefind only generates its index during the production build, so the search component shows a graceful fallback message in dev (`// search index not built yet — run npm run build first`). To preview search locally, run `npm run build && npm run preview` instead of `npm run dev`. The search will work against the built index.

faq.q: Can I customize how search results look?
faq.a: Yes. The `SearchBox.astro` component is the entire UI — about 100 lines, all editable. Results render as anchor tags with title + excerpt; styling lives in `global.css` under `.search-result-*` classes. The Pagefind API returns rich data (full text, meta, anchors) that you can use to build any UI you want.

faq.q: Is search results ranking accurate for technical terms?
faq.a: Yes. Pagefind tokenizes content with awareness of camelCase, snake_case, and other technical conventions. Searching for `useEffect` matches the term even if it appears inside `useEffect()`. Searching for `event-loop` matches both `event-loop` and `event loop` variants. Results are ranked by term frequency and title-vs-body weighting (titles count more).

variants.ctaCopy: Pagefind ships in the free version. After your next build, visit /blog and try the search — it's already working.

Solo developers who want utility-first styling without manually maintaining a design token system. Tailwind handles the spacing scale, color tokens, and breakpoints.

CSS-first config (no tailwind.config.js to maintain)
@apply still works for component classes when you need them
Theme tokens via CSS variables, consumable by Tailwind via arbitrary values

faq.q: Do I need to write Tailwind, or can I write regular CSS?
faq.a: Both work, side by side. faq.q: Will the bundled CSS get huge with all these utilities?
faq.a: No — Tailwind tree-shakes aggressively. Only utilities you actually reference in your template files end up in the build. faq.q: How does Tailwind 4 differ from Tailwind 3?

Most v3 sites can migrate to v4 with minor CSS adjustments.

faq.q: Can I add Tailwind plugins like Typography or Forms?
faq.a: Yes — Tailwind plugins still work in v4. Install via npm, import in your CSS via `@plugin '@tailwindcss/typography'`. The template doesn't ship with these enabled by default to keep the dependency tree light, but they're documented as opt-in additions in the Pro docs.

variants.ctaCopy: The free version of ClaudeStack ships with Tailwind 4 already configured. Clone, write, deploy — no setup overhead.

variants.h1: Astro + MDX Blog Template
variants.metaTitle: Astro MDX Blog Template | ClaudeStack
variants.metaDescription: An Astro 6 blog template with first-class MDX — Markdown plus embedded components. 100/100 Lighthouse. Free on GitHub or Pro at $49.
variants.intro: MDX is the killer feature for technical blogs: write Markdown for prose, drop in Astro or React components when you need interactivity, embed code snippets that render with proper syntax highlighting. ClaudeStack uses @astrojs/mdx (the official integration) configured as the default content format. Every post is an `.mdx` file; every component in `src/components/` is importable directly into post content. The frontmatter is typed via Zod so your IDE catches schema errors. Syntax highlighting comes from Shiki with the github-dark theme. The combination is the most ergonomic way to write technical content in 2026 — Markdown's brevity, JSX's flexibility, TypeScript's safety, all in one file format.
useCases: Tutorial sites with interactive demos. Drop a `<CodeSandbox>` or custom interactive component into MDX prose to show the concept in action.
Documentation that needs structured callouts, comparison tables, or version-specific tabs. MDX makes these first-class instead of requiring inline HTML.
Multi-author technical blogs where consistent layout matters. MDX components ensure every author renders code blocks, callouts, and warnings the same way.
features: @astrojs/mdx official integration, actively maintained
Shiki syntax highlighting in all code fences (200+ languages)
Frontmatter typed via Zod — schema errors caught at build, not runtime
Import any Astro component directly into MDX (no special syntax)
Astro 6 + MDX 5 — current stable versions

faq.q: Why MDX instead of plain Markdown?
faq.a: MDX is a superset of Markdown — anything that works in plain Markdown works in MDX. The difference is MDX lets you embed components when needed. There's no downside to starting in plain Markdown-flavored MDX and adding components later as use cases emerge. The template handles both indistinguishably; the file extension is `.mdx` either way.

faq.q: Can I use React components in MDX?
faq.a: Yes — install `@astrojs/react` and React components can be imported into MDX files. The template doesn't ship with React enabled (to keep the bundle clean), but enabling it is `npx astro add react`. Once added, you choose per-component whether they hydrate client-side via the `client:` directives.

faq.q: How does Astro's MDX compare to Next.js MDX?
faq.a: Functionally similar — same syntax, same import patterns. Astro's MDX is typically faster to build (Astro's island architecture is lighter than Next's server components). Astro's MDX defaults to zero client-side JavaScript; Next.js ships React runtime even for static content. For content-heavy sites, Astro's MDX yields lighter pages out of the box.

faq.q: Will MDX work for non-technical content?
faq.a: Yes — most MDX content is just enhanced Markdown. The component embedding is opt-in. Personal blogs, newsletter archives, and editorial sites use MDX successfully without ever embedding a component. The format gives you the option without forcing you to use it.

variants.ctaCopy: ClaudeStack is MDX-first by default. Clone, write your first .mdx, embed a component — it works.

variants.h1: Astro + TypeScript Blog Template
variants.metaTitle: Astro TypeScript Blog Template (Strict Mode) | ClaudeStack
variants.metaDescription: An Astro 6 blog template with TypeScript strict mode enabled. Typed frontmatter via Zod. 100/100 Lighthouse. Free on GitHub or Pro at $49.
variants.intro: TypeScript on a blog template is overkill until the moment you make your first mistake — a renamed frontmatter field that quietly broke five pages, a typo in a tag that creates a phantom tag-page, an import path that worked locally but failed in CI. ClaudeStack ships with TypeScript strict mode enabled by default, frontmatter typed via Zod, and content collections that surface schema errors at build time. The result: most of the silly mistakes get caught before the site ships, and refactoring (renaming components, restructuring data) is dramatically less scary. For solo developers it's nice; for teams it's load-bearing.
useCases: Teams where multiple people contribute. TypeScript catches the schema-drift mistakes that would otherwise show up in production.
Solo developers who want refactoring safety. Renaming a frontmatter field becomes a one-step refactor; missing references surface immediately in the editor.
Long-lived blogs where the codebase evolves over years. Type checks catch outdated assumptions when dependencies change or components are restructured.
features: TypeScript strict mode — no any, no implicit returns, no loose types
Frontmatter schema enforced via Zod (runtime + type-level validation)
Astro's content collection types auto-generated from your schema
VSCode autocomplete on every frontmatter field, every component prop
tsconfig.json extends astro/tsconfigs/strict — the most rigorous standard

faq.q: Do I need to be fluent in TypeScript to use this?
faq.a: No. Most users write articles in MDX (just Markdown plus frontmatter), and never touch TypeScript directly. The template's TypeScript usage is in the framework code (components, layouts, route handlers), which you typically don't modify. When you do customize, the editor's autocomplete and inline errors guide you — TypeScript is more helpful than annoying for non-experts.

faq.q: Will strict mode cause friction when adding components?
faq.a: Some, especially if you're used to non-strict JavaScript. The most common friction point is component props — strict mode requires explicit types. The template's existing components are examples to copy from. Once you've added 2-3 components yourself, the pattern feels natural. The catch-mistakes-early benefit outweighs the typing-overhead cost.

faq.q: Can I run TypeScript checks as part of CI?
faq.a: Yes — `npx astro check` runs the full TypeScript check (plus Astro-specific checks like template syntax). Add it to your GitHub Actions workflow as a required check before merging. Most contributors discover this after their first PR breaks the build because of a type error; running checks locally before pushing becomes the default afterward.

faq.q: Does TypeScript add to the bundle size?
faq.a: No. TypeScript compiles to JavaScript at build time; the runtime bundle contains no TypeScript-specific code. The type annotations and runtime checks via Zod do add slight build-time overhead (typically 200-500ms on a 50-post blog), but no runtime cost. Lighthouse performance scores are unaffected.

variants.ctaCopy: TypeScript strict is on by default in the free template. Get type-safe content collection from your first post.

variants.h1: Astro + Cloudflare Blog Template
variants.metaTitle: Astro Cloudflare Pages Blog Template | ClaudeStack
variants.metaDescription: An Astro blog template designed to deploy on Cloudflare Pages / Workers. Zero hosting cost. 100/100 Lighthouse. Free on GitHub or Pro at $49.
variants.intro: Cloudflare Pages (now part of Cloudflare Workers static assets) is the cheapest and fastest way to host a static blog: global CDN, unlimited bandwidth, free tier that covers any non-viral blog, automatic HTTPS, zero cold-start latency. ClaudeStack is configured for this exact stack — `wrangler.jsonc` tells Cloudflare to deploy as static assets, `_redirects` handles URL migrations, the build script chains Pagefind so search works in production. Connect a GitHub repo to Cloudflare Pages, push, and the site is live in 60 seconds. No DevOps, no server bills, no surprise charges if a post goes viral.
useCases: Solo developers who want a blog that costs less than coffee. Cloudflare's free tier handles 99% of blogs forever.
Indie hackers who want to deploy a blog alongside their product without adding infrastructure complexity. Cloudflare handles both at scale.
Open source maintainers who want zero hosting overhead. Sponsors don't have to fund a hosting bill that grows with project popularity.
features: wrangler.jsonc pre-configured for static-asset-only deploy
public/_redirects file ready for 301s when you reorganize URLs
Build chain works on Cloudflare's CI (Node 22, npm, fast deploys)
Cloudflare Web Analytics integration ready (one config edit)
Custom domain trivially configured via Cloudflare Registrar

faq.q: Pages or Workers — which should I use?
faq.a: Cloudflare is unifying both under Workers with static assets. The template uses the Workers static-assets pattern (the newer one). For pure-static sites like a blog, both Pages and Workers static-assets behave identically. Workers-with-static-assets is the recommended path going forward; Pages is in maintenance mode.

faq.q: What's the cost ceiling for a viral post?
faq.a: Effectively zero. Cloudflare's free tier on Workers includes 100,000 requests per day per project, with unlimited bandwidth. A blog post that goes mildly viral (50k-200k visits in a day) still falls within free tier. Even sustained popular sites typically stay under the paid tier threshold. You'd need to be Notion-scale to hit serious billing.

faq.q: Will my custom domain work easily?
faq.a: Yes. If you buy the domain through Cloudflare Registrar (the cheapest registrar — no markup over wholesale), DNS auto-configures. Other registrars require a one-line nameserver change. Custom Domains in the Workers dashboard takes about 30 seconds to set up; HTTPS provisions automatically within minutes.

faq.q: Can I deploy elsewhere too?
faq.a: Yes. The template makes no Cloudflare-specific assumptions in the application code. Vercel, Netlify, GitHub Pages, even self-hosted via nginx — all work with `npm run build` outputting to `dist/`. The Cloudflare-specific files (`wrangler.jsonc`, `_redirects`) are ignored by other hosts. You can deploy to multiple targets simultaneously if you want.

variants.ctaCopy: Deploy free in 60 seconds: clone, connect to Cloudflare, push. The hosting cost stays at zero forever.
